title: Remote Board Meetings and Board Monitoring Effectiveness: Evidence from China
abstract: Using Chinese data, we examine whether synchronous remote board meetings, which facilitate status equalization among directors and alleviate their pressure for conformity, affect board monitoring effectiveness. We find that compared to face-to-face meetings, synchronous remote meetings are associated with directors’ better meeting attendance behavior, a higher likelihood of director dissent on monitoring-related proposals, higher forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity, and more effective investments. These results hold when we use remote meetings that include both synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings. Proposal-director level analysis further shows that remote meetings reduce the pressure to conform faced by young first-term directors and socially connected directors.

author.Xinni Cai: Business School, University of International Business and Economics, Beijing, China
author.Fuxiu Jiang: School of Business, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China
author.Jun-Koo Kang*: Nanyang Business School, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore

article.1. Institutional Background in China:
JEL G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G30 - General G32 - Financing Policy; Financial Risk and Risk Management; Capital and Ownership Structure; Value of Firms; Goodwill G34 - Mergers; Acquisitions; Restructuring; Corporate Governance Issue Section: Article Editor: Wei Jiang Wei Jiang Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

As vigilant protectors of shareholders’ interests, boards of directors are expected to play an important role in monitoring corporate managers (Fama and Jensen 1983;Weisbach 1988). However, several constraints, including CEO influence over the process of director selection, social connections between the CEO and independent directors, director busyness, and the geographic proximity of directors, make it difficult for independent directors to play an effective monitoring role. Thus, the central issue in the literature on boards of directors has been investigating how firms optimally structure the board to help alleviate such constraints. For example, many studies examine the effects of board independence on firm performance and policies, whereas others focus on the importance of board diversity with regard to improving firm operation and value.1

However, the effectiveness of board monitoring can be influenced by several other factors. In particular, board meeting type (i.e., remote vs face-to-face board meetings) that influences the discussion environment and communication processes can significantly affect board monitoring effectiveness and corporate policy outcomes.2For example, board meeting type can affect independent directors’ time constraints to attend meetings, their incentives to participate actively in the discussion and engage in frank communication, and their willingness to disagree with CEOs and other senior directors, thereby influencing board monitoring effectiveness. In this study, we investigate this unexplored issue of the effect of board meeting type on board monitoring effectiveness by using unique board meeting data from China. Specifically, we examine why firms hold remote meetings and how such meetings affect directors’ board meeting attendance behavior, their monitoring incentives and effectiveness, and firm value.

The literature provides well-established experimental evidence on how communication modes (i.e., face-to-face meetings vs. remote meetings) influence communication processes and decision quality (Hiltz, Johnson, and Turoff 1986;Siegel et al. 1986;Dubrovsky, Kiesler, and Sethna 1991;Mobius and Rosenblat 2006;Ho and McLeod 2008;Rhoads 2010;Eddy 2019). For example, because face-to-face meetings allow attendants to convey communication signals immediately without any lag, to communicate directly by talking with and listening to each other, and to observe the nonverbal behavior of other attendants, they tend to be particularly effective in situations that require a comprehensive understanding of the issues and for social-emotional communication (Daft and Lengel 1986;Hiltz, Johnson, and Turoff 1986;Warkentin, Sayeed, and Hightower 1997;Riordan and Kreuz 2010;Eddy 2019).

However, face-to-face meetings also have disadvantages. First, since attendants are physically present at face-to-face meetings, they can observe each other. Thus, the visual signals and social context cues (e.g., facial expression, vocal tone, body language, body posture, and gestures) they convey remind them of the physical differences and status hierarchy among them. This can compel attendants to yield to group pressure (Siegel et al. 1986;Dubrovsky, Kiesler, and Sethna 1991;Magee and Galinsky 2008), thus discouraging their objective judgment, active participation, frank communication, and willingness to disagree with other attendants (Asch 1955;Kiesler, Siegel, and McGuire 1984). Moreover, face-to-face meetings can entail significant costs for both firms and attendants due to time spent on long-distance travel, high travel costs, and other financial costs associated with such meetings (Campbell 1998;Ferran and Watts 2008).

These concerns tend to be substantially alleviated in remote meetings, at which attendants discuss agendas without facing each other. For example, in remote meetings, the observability of social context cues is significantly reduced,3which helps promote status equalization and balanced participation (Kiesler, Siegel, and McGuire 1984;Siegel et al. 1986;Sproull and Kiesler 1986;Dubrovsky, Kiesler, and Sethna 1991;Ho and McLeod 2008;Mejias et al. 2018). Moreover, remote meetings that involve few visual images and contextual cues decrease the pressure for conformity and encourage participants to voice unpopular opinions and disagree directly with others (Deutsch and Gerard 1955;Smilowitz, Compton, and Flint 1988;Adrianson and Hjelmquist 1991;Roos, Postmes, and Koudenburg 2020). Remote meetings can also reduce attendants’ travel time and costs and thus decrease spatial and temporal limitations, thereby improving participants’ meeting attendance behavior and the effectiveness of their discussions and decisions, particularly in cases of urgent or frequently held meetings.

These arguments suggest that although directors who attend face-to-face meetings can exchange their information and opinions in a rich way, the presence of insiders and other senior directors—including top management and representatives of shareholders who have a strong influence on directors’ nomination/appointment—may constantly remind directors of their inferior status and the benefits they obtain from these insiders and senior directors. Thus, their monitoring incentives are weakened in face-to-face meetings. In contrast, remote meetings allow attendants to focus more closely on the agenda itself rather than on social context cues, to think more independently, and to feel less pressured when they disagree with high-hierarchy directors and CEOs at the meetings. Thus, their monitoring incentives and capabilities are strengthened in remote meetings. In particular, to the extent that monitoring-related proposals (i.e., proposals that require more effective monitoring by directors) are more likely to be influenced by insiders and controlling shareholders and require more subjective judgments from directors than other types of proposals (Malenko 2013), remote meetings, which provide an environment that is more conducive to such judgments, are likely to be better suited to monitoring-related proposals and can consequently help improve board monitoring effectiveness.

Although the lack of direct communication in remote meetings could affect board monitoring effectiveness adversely,4directors attending remote meetings receive relevant materials related to the proposals prior to the meetings. By contacting the management team or board secretary any time before the meeting date, the directors can also obtain other proposal-specific information to clarify the relevant issues. These procedures ensure that regardless of the meetings they attend, directors are fully informed of all meeting-related issues, including proposal-specific details.5Thus, remote meetings do not necessarily entail a lack of information content that directors must have to vote in an informed manner. Consistent with this view, a recent study on virtual shareholder meetings (VSMs) byBrochet, Chychyla, and Ferri (2021)shows that VSMs do not cause a loss in information content or reflect an attempt to avoid scrutiny from shareholders.

Overall, these arguments suggest that although remote meetings may be less effective at facilitating direct communication among meeting attendants than face-to-face meetings, the unique characteristics of remote meetings (i.e., the reduced influence of the social hierarchy and the decreased pressure to conform) and directors’ legal responsibilities and reputational concerns help mitigate the potential problem of decreased information flow at remote meetings and increase directors’ monitoring incentives.

We therefore predict that remote meetings improve board monitoring effectiveness. To test this prediction, we focus on director dissent, which reflects directors’ willingness to confront insiders (Jiang, Wan, and Zhao 2016), and forced CEO turnover, which is considered to be one of the most aggressive actions taken by a board of directors (e.g.,Weisbach 1988). Specifically, we hypothesize that the likelihood of dissent on monitoring-related proposals and the sensitivity of forced CEO turnover to performance are higher in remote meetings than in face-to-face meetings. To the extent that remote meetings reduce directors’ travel time and costs and thus improve their meeting attendance behavior, we also hypothesize that the proportion of directors who attend a meeting is higher for remote meetings than for face-to-face meetings.

We test these predictions using a large sample of board meetings and proposals of Chinese firms included in the CSI300 stock index from 2006 to 2019. We use Chinese firms because data concerning the form of each board meeting are available only in China. Unlike the United States, where no rules mandate the public disclosure of details on board meeting forms and agendas, in 2004, China mandated that all listed firms publicly disclose information regarding meeting forms and director voting for each proposal. We compile this comprehensive information on board meetings by collecting it manually from public notices on resolutions of board meetings held by the sample firms.

We first conduct the analyses using a subsample of synchronous board meetings which includes remote meetings held via video or telephone (i.e., synchronous remote meetings) and face-to-face meetings. Given that real-time discussion among directors is absent at asynchronous remote meetings held via email or fax, the exclusion of these meetings from the analyses allows us to focus on meetings in which directors actively participate in collaborative discussions. As a first test, we examine the likelihood of holding a synchronous remote meeting. A priori, why boards hold synchronous remote meetings is unclear. Because synchronous remote meetings save directors’ travel time and costs, boards are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings to discuss urgent agendas that require quick, timely decisions. For example, if a board wants to respond quickly to a case of poor performance, it is more likely to hold a synchronous remote meeting than to take the extra time to schedule a face-to-face meeting. Boards may also hold a synchronous remote meeting for reasons of director power/convenience or management power/convenience. Powerful directors may request that firms hold synchronous remote meetings to avoid the inconvenience of attending face-to-face meetings. Alternatively, if managers want the board to monitor them less intensely or if they want to keep directors less informed, they may prefer synchronous remote meetings. We find that urgency and director power/convenience, not management power/convenience, are the primary motivations for synchronous remote meetings: the likelihood of holding a synchronous remote meeting is higher for firms experiencing large decreases in performance, and it increases as the number of days from the board meeting notice date to the board meeting date decreases. Synchronous remote meetings are also more likely to be held by boards with more influential directors or boards with directors who require more convenience, such as boards with more nonlocal directors and boards with longer director tenures.

Next, we examine the impact of synchronous remote meetings on the monitoring effectiveness of independent directors. First, using board meeting-level data, we examine whether synchronous remote meetings improve directors’ board meeting attendance behavior. We find that the proportion of independent directors who attend a board meeting is significantly higher for synchronous remote meetings than for face-to-face meetings. Thus, synchronous remote meetings improve the board meeting attendance of independent directors. Second, using proposal-level data, we examine how independent directors’ voting behavior at synchronous remote meetings differs from that at face-to-face board meetings. We find that compared to directors who attend face-to-face meetings, those who attend synchronous remote meetings are more likely to dissent on proposals concerning monitoring-related issues. This result supports our hypothesis that directors at synchronous remote meetings face less pressure for conformity and exhibit more willingness to disagree than those at face-to-face meetings. Third, using board meeting-level data, we examine whether synchronous remote meetings are related to higher forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity. Consistent with our hypothesis, we find that the sensitivity of forced CEO turnover to past performance is higher in synchronous remote meetings than in face-to-face meetings.

To alleviate endogeneity concerns associated with potential self-selection bias inherent in our study, we estimate aHeckman (1979)treatment effect model in which we use the extreme severe weather condition around the meeting date as the identifying restriction in the first stage. To further address endogeneity concerns, we conduct several additional analyses, including propensity score matching analyses, firm-by-board fixed effects regressions, and subsample analyses based on director power/convenience and corporate governance. Our results do not change. Overall, these results support our hypothesis that synchronous remote board meetings help improve board monitoring effectiveness.

Finally, we examine whether the valuation effects of mergers and acquisitions (M&As) differ between those approved at synchronous remote meetings and those approved at face-to-face meetings. If synchronous remote meetings allow directors to play a more effective monitoring role than face-to-face meetings and if this improved monitoring effectiveness leads to higher firm value, firms whose M&A proposals, particularly those involving a related party (RPT M&As) that require more effective monitoring, are approved at a synchronous remote meeting should experience a larger increase in value than firms whose M&A proposals are approved at a face-to-face meeting. Consistent with this prediction, we find that acquirer M&A announcement returns are more positive whenRPT M&Aproposals are approved at synchronous remote meetings rather than at face-to-face meetings.

We then repeat all the analyses after replacing synchronous remote meetings with remote meetings that include synchronous remote meetings held via video or telephone, asynchronous remote meetings held via email or fax, and remote meetings with unknown type. We find that our results do not change. In addition, by using proposal-director-level data and controlling for proposal fixed effects to address the endogeneity problems associated with board formation and proposal selection, we find that remote meetings not only help independent directors with stronger career concerns (i.e., young first-term directors) dissent more but also reduce the pressure to conform faced by socially connected directors, who tend to experience difficulties in disagreeing with CEOs and other top management.

Our paper contributes to the literature in at least two important ways. First, our paper contributes to the literature on board monitoring effectiveness. Although many countries allow board meetings to be held remotely, thus far, no data regarding the form of board meetings are publicly available to study the differences between (synchronous) remote and face-to-face meetings. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to examine this issue. Unlike previous studies that have mainly focused on board structure (e.g.,Hermalin and Weisbach 2003) or board diversity (e.g.,Bernile, Bhagwat, and Yonker 2018) to examine board monitoring effectiveness, our study identifies board meeting form as another important factor that affects board monitoring effectiveness and show that (synchronous) remote meetings improve directors’ monitoring effectiveness and firm value, particularly for firms whose proposals require greater monitoring by independent directors. We also examine the motivations underlying boards’ decisions to hold (synchronous) remote meetings and show that urgency and director power/convenience are the primary motivations.

Second, our study extends the literature on the differences in social status hierarchy and pressure for conformity between face-to-face and remote communications. Most studies that examine this issue employ a laboratory or field study approach (e.g.,Hiltz, Johnson, and Turoff 1986). Although these studies enhance our understanding of the differences between face-to-face and remote communications, no previous study examines their differences using a large sample of director-, meeting-, proposal-, and firm-level observations. Supporting the evidence in previous experimental studies, we show that (synchronous) remote board meetings result in better director monitoring, possibly by reducing the influence of social status hierarchy and the pressure for conformity.
As with firms in other countries, boards of directors are an essential part of Chinese firms. The Chinese Company Law requires the board of directors to comprise 5 to 19 directors, one-third of whom should be independent directors from unrelated parties. Similar to those in the U.S. firms, the boards of directors in Chinese firms perform two important functions: monitoring and advising (Fama and Jensen 1983;Hermalin and Weisbach 2003;Adams and Ferreira 2009). Moreover, in both countries, board meetings represent important contexts in which directors perform their main duties by discussing and approving various corporate strategies and proposals (Jiang, Wan, and Zhao 2016).

Most states in the United States allow firms to hold remote board meetings as long as all directors can hear each other simultaneously.6Similarly, China does not stipulate any specific requirements concerning the form of board meetings. However, while the United States does not mandate the disclosure of board meeting forms, China does have such a mandate.7The Chinese two stock exchanges adopted a revised version of the “Rules Governing the Listing of Stocks on Shanghai (Shenzhen) Stock Exchange” on December 10, 2004, and the revised rules mandate public disclosure of the meeting notice date, the date it is held, its location and form, and the attendance record and voting behavior of each independent director. These rules enable us to collect comprehensive data on board meetings, especially regarding the form of board meetings, the specific proposals discussed at each meeting, and directors’ voting behavior, and to examine how these meeting-specific variables are related to monitoring outcomes and firm value.

Previous studies have documented that although the pressure for conformity and status hierarchy exist in Western countries (e.g.,Deutsch and Gerard 1955;Dubrovsky, Kiesler, and Sethna 1991), such pressure is more severe in China (Hwang 1987;Leung et al. 2011). Unlike Western countries, where individualism is highly valued, China has been strongly influenced by Confucianism (i.e., a cultural tradition of people’s desire for interpersonal harmony and conformity and avoidance of conflicts among people) (Kirkbride, Tang, and Westwood 1991;Morris et al. 1998). In addition, hierarchically structured networks of social relationships and social positions created by a favorable image in front of others (Mianzi) are a fundamental part of Chinese culture. These cultural traditions have encouraged Chinese people to consider direct confrontation with others (e.g., negotiation and debate) undesirable because such confrontation is likely to hurt others’ feelings and disrupt stable interpersonal relationships (Hwang 1987;Leung et al. 2011). Thus, the social status hierarchy and pressure for conformity are likely to be much more notable in China than in Western countries. Consequently, the positive influence of remote board meetings on attendants’ interactions (e.g., allowing participants to engage in equal, independent communication and to express disagreements more freely) should be more pronounced in China.
article.2. Sample, Variable Definition, and Summ:
2.1 Sample

To construct our sample, we start with firms included in the CSI300 stock index from 2005 to 2019.8The sample period begins in 2005 because the requirement for the disclosure of board meeting form and the construction of the CSI300 index began in 2004 and 2005, respectively. However, since we control for the frequency that the firm held board meetings during the previous year (Board meeting number) in all regression analyses and information onBoard meeting numberis available only from 2006, we exclude all observations in 2005 from the analyses. If the firms are listed on the index only for certain years during our sample period, we include their unlisted years in our sample firm-years. For these firms, we manually collect board meeting-specific information from the public notices on resolutions of board meetings announced on the official websites of the Shanghai and Shenzhen Stock Exchanges. We exclude firms for which board meeting data are unavailable, firms in financial industries, and firms with missing stock returns and financial data in the China Securities Market and Accounting Research (CSMAR) database.9Because the information on board meeting form is incomplete for the firms’ listing and delisting years, we also delete these years from the sample. In addition, we exclude meetings in which some directors attend remotely and other directors attend face-to-face because of the mixed nature of the meeting characteristics. Our final sample consists of 58,133 board meetings (282,553 board meeting-proposals) held by 711 firms, of which 29,323 (201,536) are synchronous board meetings (synchronous board meeting-proposals) held by 706 firms. Data on institutional ownership and M&As are obtained from the Chinese Research Data Services Platform (CNRDS) and the CSMAR database, respectively.

2.2 Variable definitions

We define a meeting as a remote board meeting if the public notice on the resolution of the board meeting states that the meeting is held via telephone, video, email, or fax or if it states that the meeting is held via telecommunication media but does not specify the exact media type. Otherwise, we define the meeting as a face-to-face meeting. We further classify remote meetings into three subgroups: synchronous remote meetings, asynchronous remote meetings, and unknown type of remote meetings. Synchronous remote meeting is a remote meeting that is held synchronously via telephone or video. Specifically, we consider a meeting as the synchronous remote meeting if the public notice clearly states that the meeting is held via telephone or video, or if it states that the meeting is held via telecommunication media and also mentions both the meeting time (e.g., morning, afternoon) and meeting location (e.g., firm’s meeting room, firm’s headquarters), or both the terms “host” (e.g., the chair hosts the meeting) and “coattending” (e.g., the supervisory board also attends the meeting).10Asynchronous remote meeting is a remote meeting for which the public notice states that the meeting is held via email or fax. Unknown type of remote meeting is a remote meeting whose communication type cannot be determined. We define the board meeting-level variable(Synchronous) Remote meeting, which equals one if the meeting is a (synchronous) remote meeting, and zero if the meeting is held face-to-face, when examining the determinants of (synchronous) remote meetings, directors’ board attendance behavior, forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity, and the market reaction to M&A announcements. We also define the board meeting-proposal-level variable(Synchronous) Remote proposal, which equals one if the proposal is discussed at a (synchronous) remote meeting, and zero if the proposal is discussed at a face-to-face meeting. We use this variable to examine the effects of remote board meetings on the likelihood of director dissent.

2.3 Summary statistics

Table 1presents the distribution of remote (face-to-face) meetings, remote (face-to-face) proposals, and firms by year and meeting characteristics. Panel A shows the distribution of remote and face-to-face meetings by year. Of the 58,133 board meetings, 31,796 (54.70%⁠) are remote meetings, and 26,337 (45.30%⁠) are face-to-face meetings. Of the 31,796 remote meetings, 2,986 are synchronous remote meetings held via telephone or video, 3,350 are asynchronous remote meetings held via email or fax, and 25,460 are remote meetings whose exact communication type is unknown.11Thus, the subsample of synchronous meetings includes 29,323 meetings, of which 2,986 are synchronous remote meetings and 26,337 are face-to-face meetings. Panel A also shows that the ratio of the number of remote meetings to the total number of board meetings held during each fiscal year increases significantly from 32.14%in 2006 to 69.77%in 2019, more than doubling over this period of 14 years. The ratio of the number of synchronous remote meetings to the total number of board meetings also more than triples from 2.38%in 2006 to 8.49%in 2019. These results suggest that the importance of remote meetings, particularly synchronous remote meetings, as a form of board meeting has increased significantly over time and that remote meetings have become as popular as face-to-face meetings.Internet Appendix Table Bfurther presents the distribution of remote and face-to-face meetings by month and industry.

The sample consists of 58,133 board meetings, 282,553 board meeting-proposal observations, and 711 unique firms. The sample is limited to firms that have ever been covered in the CSI300 stock index from 2006 to 2019. We exclude firms that operate in financial industries from the sample. Remote meeting refers to a board meeting that is held via telecommunication media, including email, fax, telephone, and video, while face-to-face meeting refers to a board meeting that is held on-site. Remote proposal refers to a proposal that is discussed at a remote meeting, and face-to-face proposal refers to a proposal that is discussed at a face-to-face meeting. Remote meetings are further divided into three subgroups by manually checking public notices on resolutions of board meetings: synchronous remote meetings, asynchronous remote meetings, and unknown type of meetings. Synchronous remote meeting is a remote meeting for which the public notice clearly states that the meeting is held via telephone or video, or it mentions both the meeting time (e.g., morning, afternoon) and meeting location (e.g., firm’s meeting room, firm’s headquarters), or both the terms “host” (e.g., the chair hosts the meeting) and “coattending” (e.g., the supervisory board also attends the meeting). Asynchronous remote meeting is a remote meeting for which the public notice states that the meeting is held via email or fax. Unknown type of meeting is a remote meeting whose communication type cannot be determined. Panel A presents the frequency distribution of remote and face-to-face meetings by year. Panel B presents the frequency distribution of remote and face-to-face proposals by proposal type. Panel C presents the frequency distribution of remote and face-to-face meetings by the number of proposals covered in each meeting. Panel D presents the distribution of firms by the proportion of meetings held remotely.

Panel B shows the distributions of remote and face-to-face proposals by proposal topic. Remote meetings are most commonly used for proposals related to guarantees (49.21%⁠) and financing and capital structure (46.42%⁠). They are least likely to be used for proposals related to payout policies (10.89%⁠) and auditing, accounting treatment, and information disclosures (14.35%⁠), which tend to be discussed primarily at annual meetings. The distribution of synchronous remote proposals exhibits similar patterns. Panel C presents the distributions of remote and face-to-face meetings by the number of proposals covered. The fraction of meetings that cover only one or two proposals (five or more proposals) is 59.68%(15.37%⁠) for remote meetings, 54.32%(19.26%⁠) for synchronous remote meetings, 61.91%(14.45%⁠) for asynchronous remote meetings, and 25.22%(53.74%⁠) for face-to-face meetings. Thus, remote (face-to-face) meetings, regardless of their communication type, are used mainly to discuss a small (large) number of proposals.12Panel D reports the proportion of firms that hold a certain number of meetings remotely. We find that firms that hold 0%(0%–25%⁠, 25%–50%⁠, 50%–75%⁠, 75%–100%⁠, 100%⁠) of their meetings remotely account for 7.45%(9.85%⁠, 24.75%⁠, 40.65%⁠, 16.32%⁠, 0.98%⁠) of the overall sample, suggesting that the remote meetings in our sample are not held exclusively by a small number of specific firms but are rather held by a large number of different firms.13

Using 58,133 board meeting observations, inTable 2, we compare sample characteristics between remote board meetings and face-to-face meetings. The detailed variable definitions used inTable 2are provided in theappendix. Several results are noteworthy. First, although the number of board meetings held in a year (Board meeting number) is larger for remote meetings than for face-to-face meetings, the number of proposals discussed at the meeting (Proposal number) and the number of days from the meeting notice date to the meeting date (Interval) are smaller for remote meetings than for face-to-face meetings. Thus, remote meetings are more likely to be held when proposals need to be discussed in a timely manner. We also find that the likelihood of experiencing a large decline in performance before the meeting (Drop in performance) is higher for firms holding remote meetings, further supporting the view that boards hold remote meetings to discuss urgent issues.14

Second, boards holding remote meetings have more nonlocal independent directors (Proportion of independent directors from other provinces), busier independent directors (Average board seats held by independent directors), and more independent directors (Proportion of independent directors) than boards holding face-to-face meetings. Thus, remote meetings are more likely to be used when directors have more power or when firms need to offer more convenience to directors.

Finally, firms holding remote meetings exhibit higher CEO-chair duality, lower return on assets (ROA), higher leverage, lower likelihood of being a state-owned enterprise (SOE), and lower ownership by the largest shareholders. All these significant differences are also observed between firms that hold synchronous remote meetings and those that hold face-to-face meetings except for an insignificant difference in leverage ratios.
article.3. Determinants of Remote Board Meetings:
To better understand the determinants of remote meetings, inTable 3, we estimate a logit (conditional logit) regression with industry (firm) fixed effects. The dependent variable in columns 1 and 2, which use the subsample of synchronous board meetings, isSynchronous remote meeting,and the dependent variable in columns 3 and 4, which use the full sample of board meetings, isRemote meeting. We classify the variables used inTable 2into three groups: urgency to hold board meetings, director power/convenience, and management power/convenience. We measure urgency usingDrop in performanceandInterval; director power/convenience usingProportionof independent directors from other provinces,Averageboard seats held by independent directors,Average tenure of independent directors, Proportion of male directors,Proportion of independent directors, andBoard size; and manager power/convenience usingCEO-chair duality,FamilyCEO,CEO ownership,CEO age, andCEO tenure. The regressions control for the board meeting characteristics and firm characteristics used inTable 2that can either directly or indirectly affect the likelihood of holding remote meetings. Specifically, we includeProposal number,Annual meeting, andBoard meeting numberas board meeting characteristics andROA,Leverage,Size,SOE, andInstitutional ownershipas firm characteristics. InInternet Appendix D, we discuss the reasons for including these variables in our regression analyses, including those inTables 3–6, in further detail.

Panel A reports estimates of regressions using board meeting-level data, in which the dependent variable is an indicator that equals one if a forced CEO turnover proposal is passed at the meeting, and zero otherwise. Columns 1–3 report results using the subsample of synchronous meetings that includes face-to-face meetings and synchronous remote meetings, and columns 4–6 report results using the full sample of meetings that includes face-to-face meetings and all types of remote meetings (i.e., synchronous remote meetings, asynchronous remote meetings, and remote meetings whose communication type is unknown). The subsample of synchronous meetings (full sample of meetings) consists of 29,223 (57,974) board meeting observations from 706 (711) unique firms. The sample is limited to firms that have ever been covered in the CSI300 stock index from 2006 to 2019. We exclude firms that operate in financial industries from the sample. Synchronous remote meeting equals one if the public notice clearly states that the meeting is held via telephone or video, or if it states that the meeting is held via telecommunication media and also mentions both the meeting time (e.g., morning, afternoon) and meeting location (e.g., firm’s meeting room, firm’s headquarters), or both the terms “host” (e.g., the chair hosts the meeting) and “coattending” (e.g., the supervisory board also attends the meeting), and zero if it states that the meeting is held face-to-face. Remote meeting equals one if the meeting is a synchronous remote meeting, if the public notice states that the meeting is an asynchronous remote meeting held via email or fax, or if the public notice states that the meeting is held via telecommunication media but does not specify the exact media type, and zero if the public notice states that the meeting is held face-to-face. Panel B reports the implied probabilities of forced CEO turnover for firms at the top and bottom quartiles (terciles) of firm performance. The implied probabilities are calculated using the coefficients of the regression in column 2 of panel A and averaged across all observations. The implied probabilities at the 10th, 25th, 75th, and 90th percentiles of firm performance are then calculated by varying only the performance variable (i.e., Past industry-adjusted ROA ) and its interaction with Remote meeting , while holding all other independent variables constant at their actual values. The appendix describes the variables used in the table in detail. The z -statistics reported in parentheses are based on standard errors clustered by firm. * p < .1 ⁠ ; ** p < .05 ⁠ ; *** p < .01 ⁠ .

We find that the coefficient forDrop in performanceis positive and significant in column 2, in which we include firm fixed effects in the regression. Thus, firms are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings when they experience a large decrease in stock performance than when the same firms do not experience such a decrease. This result is similar when we useRemote meetingas the dependent variable in columns 3 and 4. We also find that the coefficient forIntervalis negative and significant in all four columns. Thus, (synchronous) remote meetings tend to be used to discuss proposals at shorter notice. These results indicate that the use of (synchronous) remote meetings helps the board quickly respond to urgent issues and thus represents a higher level of board monitoring intensity than the use of face-to-face meetings.

With regard to director power/convenience, in column 1 in which we use industry fixed effects, we find that firms with a higher proportion of independent directors from other provinces are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings. To the extent that nonlocal directors face greater difficulties in physically showing up at face-to-face meetings than local directors,15this result suggests that director power/convenience is another reasonable motive for holding remote meetings. In column 2, in which we replace industry fixed effects with firm fixed effects, we find that firms are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings when their independent directors have longer tenures. To the extent that longer-tenured directors have stronger power than shorter-tenured directors, this result further supports the argument identifying director power/convenience as a reason underlying a firm’s choice to hold a synchronous remote meeting. When we use the full sample of board meetings, consistent with the findings in columns 1 and 2, the coefficient forProportion of independent directors from other provincesin columns 3 and 4 and the coefficient forAverage tenure of independent directorsin column 4 are positive and significant. We also find that the coefficient forProportion of male directorsin column 3 is positive and significant. Since male directors in China tend to have a stronger voice than female directors due to the country’s unique Confucian culture, which demands women’s obedience, this result further suggests that firms use remote meetings to facilitate the attendance of powerful directors. The positive and significant coefficient forBoard sizein columns 3 and 4 further supports the claim that firms are more likely to hold remote meetings when they need to offer convenience to directors since it is difficult for all directors to participate in face-to-face meetings when the board size is larger.

With regard to manager power/convenience, we find that none of the variables that measure manager power/convenience explains the likelihood of holding remote meetings except forCEO-chair dualityin column 3 andCEO tenurein column 4. Although a positive and significant coefficient forCEO-chair dualityis consistent with the predictions of the manager power/convenience argument,CEO-chair dualitycan also measure director power/convenience since board chairs who serve as CEOs have more power in influencing the choice to hold remote meetings.16The negative and significant coefficient forCEO tenurecontradicts the prediction of the manager power/convenience argument because CEOs with longer tenures tend to have stronger power than those with shorter tenures.

Turning to board meetings and firm characteristics, the negative and significant coefficient forProposal numberin columns 1 and 2 suggests that firms are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings when they have fewer proposals to discuss. We also find that the coefficient forAnnual meetingis negative and significant in these two columns, suggesting that the likelihood of holding annual meetings as synchronous remote meetings is low. We further find that the coefficient forBoard meeting numberis positive and significant in column 2. Thus, firms are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings when they held more board meetings the previous year than when they held fewer board meetings the previous year. All these results hold for the regressions in columns 3 and 4. Finally, in columns 3 and 4, the coefficient forLeverageis positive and significant, suggesting that high leverage induces firms to rely more on remote meetings to enable them to be more responsive to urgent issues.

Overall, these results indicate that firms are more likely to use (synchronous) remote meetings when they need to make quick, timely decisions regarding urgent issues and when their boards have more influential directors or directors who require more convenience, such as boards with more nonlocal directors and boards whose independent directors have longer tenures, suggesting that urgency and director power/convenience are the primary motivations for firms to hold remote meetings.
article.4. Impact of Remote Board Meetings on Bo:
To investigate how remote board meetings affect board monitoring effectiveness, in this section, we focus on three types of board monitoring activities, namely, independent directors’ board meeting attendance behavior, dissent votes, and forced CEO turnover activities.

4.1 Independent directors’ board meeting attendance behavior

First, we investigate whether independent directors’ board meeting attendance behavior differs between remote and face-to-face meetings. We focus on director meeting attendance because it is a direct measure of director performance and the main channel through which directors obtain the information necessary to carry out their duties and exert influence over managers (Adams and Ferreira 2009). We estimate a tobit regression using board meeting-level data, in which the dependent variable is the ratio of the number of independent directors who attend the board meeting to the total number of independent directors on the board (Proportion of independent directors attended). We control for the board meeting characteristics used inTable 3, includingProposal number,Annual meeting, andBoard meeting number, since these variables are found to be important determinants of the choice of remote meetings and are also likely to influence directors’ meeting attendance behavior. We further control for various board-specific characteristics, includingProportionof independent directors from other provinces,Averageboard seats held by independent directors,Proportionof maledirectors,Proportion of independent directors,Board size, andCEO-chair duality. Following (Adams and Ferreira (2008,2009) andMasulis and Mobbs (2014), we also control for several firm characteristics, including firm performance (ROA), growth opportunity (Tobin’s q), and firm size (Size). Given the differences in corporate governance between SOEs and non-SOEs and the prevalence of controlling shareholders in China (Jiang and Kim 2020), we additionally control for an indicator for SOEs and ownership held by the largest shareholder (Top-1 ownership). Finally, we control for year, month, and industry (firm) fixed effects to alleviate the concern that seasonality, time trends, and time-invariant omitted industry (firm) characteristics drive our results.

Table 4reports the results. In columns 1–3 in which we use the subsample of synchronous board meetings, the key independent variable isSynchronous remote meeting. In column 1, we control for all variables and include industry fixed effects. We find that the coefficient forSynchronous remote meetingis positive and significant, suggesting that independent directors’ attendance records are better in synchronous remote meetings than in face-to-face meetings. The marginal effect ofSynchronous remote meetingis 0.1, indicating thatProportion of independent directorsattendedis 10%higher in synchronous remote meetings than in face-to-face meetings. Given that the unconditional average proportion of independent directors who attend the synchronous meeting is 93%and that all independent directors attend 79.98%of these meetings, the marginal effect of 0.1 appears to be economically large. In column 2, we replace industry fixed effects in column 1 with firm fixed effects. Our results do not change. These results support our hypothesis that synchronous remote meetings enhance board monitoring effectiveness.

In columns 4–6, we reestimate the regressions in columns 1–3 using the full sample of board meetings. The key independent variable isRemote meeting. We find that the coefficient forRemote meetingis positive and significant in all three regressions.

4.2 Likelihood of dissent votes

To directly examine how directors behave differently between remote and face-to-face meetings, we next focus on independent directors’ voting behavior. Directors can cast one of the three types of votes for each proposal: “for,” “abstain,” and “against.” We classify both “abstain” and “against” votes as dissent votes and use them to measure director disagreement with the proposal.Jiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016), who examine the voting behavior of independent directors in Chinese firms from 2004 to 2012, argue that dissent votes reflect directors’ willingness to confront management and have a significant influence on public reactions. Thus, dissent votes should capture directors’ monitoring incentives and their willingness to exert influence over managers in a more direct way than other measures. Similar to the dissent rate of 0.1%shown inJiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016), we find that of 282,553 proposals, 341 receive dissent votes (123 “against” and 218 “abstain” votes) from independent directors.18

Because not all proposals discussed at the board meeting necessarily require board monitoring and the objective of this test is to examine how remote meetings can help alleviate directors’ pressure for conformity and thus encourage them to engage actively in monitoring activities, we focus on a specific type of proposal in which controlling shareholders and managers are more likely to extract private benefits at the expense of other shareholders, namely, related-party transactions (Lo, Wong, and Firth 2010).19Specifically, we define a variableMonitoring-related RPT proposal, which equals one if the proposal regards transactions with any related parties, and zero otherwise. We also consider more broadly defined monitoring-related proposals (Monitoring-related all proposal), including proposals regarding auditing, accounting treatment, and information disclosure, those regarding director and officer selection, appointment, and turnover, and those regarding managerial compensation in addition toMonitoring-related RPT proposal. We consider these proposals as monitoring-related proposals because they are discussed at the committees in which board independence is required by law.20We then use the interaction ofMonitoring-related RPT proposal(Monitoring-related all proposal) withSynchronous remote proposalas our key independent variable of interest.21

Panel A ofTable 5shows the likelihood of dissent estimated using logit and conditional logit regressions. Columns 1–6 use the subsample of proposals discussed at synchronous meetings. Columns 1–3 and columns 4–6 useMonitoring-related RPT proposalandMonitoring-related all proposal, respectively, as the measure of monitoring-related proposals. Columns 1, 2, 4, and 5 use proposal-level data when estimating the regressions, in which the dependent variable is an indicator that equals one if there is at least one dissent vote on the proposal by independent directors, and zero otherwise (Dissent). The regressions control for meeting-, board-, and firm-specific characteristics used in column 1 ofTable 4. FollowingJiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016)andMa and Khanna (2016), we also control forLeverageandInstitutional ownership, which are likely to affect the likelihood of dissent. We further include proposal category fixed effects in all regressions to control for the differences in topics across proposals. We find that the coefficients for the interaction term betweenMonitoring-related RPT proposal(Monitoring-related all proposal) andSynchronous remote proposalare positive and significant in columns 1, 2, 4, and 5. In column 1, the marginal effects ofMonitoring-related RPT proposaland its interaction withSynchronous remote proposalare−0.0002 and 0.003, respectively, suggesting that the likelihood of dissent is 0.02%(0.28%⁠) lower (higher) for related-party transaction proposals than for other proposals when the proposals are discussed at face-to-face meetings (synchronous remote meetings). Given that the unconditional likelihood of dissent is 0.11%for the subsample of synchronous meetings, this difference of 0.3%in the likelihood of dissent is economically large and significant.

Panel A reports results from proposal-level analyses with industry (firm) fixed effects and proposal-director-level analyses with firm-by-director fixed effects. In panel A, columns 1–6 report results using the subsample of proposals discussed at synchronous meetings that includes face-to-face meetings and synchronous remote meetings, and columns 7–12 report results using the full sample of proposals that includes proposals discussed at face-to-face meetings and those discussed at remote meetings (i.e., synchronous remote meetings, asynchronous remote meetings, or remote meetings whose communication type is unknown). Panel B reports results of proposal-director-level analyses with proposal fixed effects using the full sample. The sample size in each column varies depending on the regression specification used. The sample is limited to firms that have ever been covered in the CSI300 stock index from 2006 to 2019. We exclude firms that operate in financial industries from the sample. In columns 1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 10, and 11 of panel A, the dependent variable is Dissent , which equals one if the proposal receives at least one “abstain” or “against” vote from an independent director, and zero otherwise. In columns 3, 6, 9, and 12 of panel A and all columns of panel B, the dependent variable is Director dissent , which equals one if the director casts an “abstain” or “against” vote on the proposal, and zero otherwise. Synchronous remote proposal equals one if the proposal is discussed at a synchronous remote meeting, and zero if the proposal is discussed at a face-to-face meeting. Remote proposal equals one if the proposal is discussed at a remote meeting, and zero if the proposal is discussed at a face-to-face meeting. Synchronous remote meeting is a remote meeting for which the public notice clearly states that the meeting is held via telephone or video, or it mentions both the meeting time (e.g., morning, afternoon) and meeting location (e.g., firm’s meeting room, firm’s headquarters), or both the terms “host” (e.g., the chair hosts the meeting) and “coattending” (e.g., the supervisory board also attends the meeting). Remote meetings include synchronous remote meetings, meetings for which the public notice states that the meeting is an asynchronous remote meeting held via email or fax, and meetings for which the public notice states that the meeting is held via telecommunication media but does not specify the exact media type. Monitoring-related RPT proposal equals one if the proposal regards a related-party transaction, and zero otherwise. Monitoring-related all proposal equals one if the proposal regards a related-party transaction, auditing, accounting treatment, information disclosure, director and officer selection, appointment, turnover, and compensation, and zero otherwise. The appendix describes the variables used in the table in detail. The z -statistics reported in parentheses are based on standard errors clustered by firm. * p < .1 ⁠ ; ** p < .05 ⁠ ; *** p < .01 ⁠ .

In columns 3 and 6, we reestimate the regressions in columns 2 and 5, respectively, using proposal-director-level data, in which the dependent variable is an indicator that equals one if the independent director dissents on the proposal, and zero otherwise (Director dissent). In addition to replacing firm fixed effects and board characteristics with firm-by-director fixed effects and director characteristics, respectively, we followJiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016)andMa and Khanna (2016)and control forDirector age, an indicator for whether the director is serving her first term on the focal firm’s board (First-term director), an indicator for whether the director is socially connected to top management or the ultimate owner (Director social tie), an indicator for male directors, director education level (Director education), an indicator for whether the director is appointed by the current chair (Director hired by chair), and indicators for the director’s professional background.22Director age,First-term director, andDirector social tiemeasure directors’ career concerns, their dual incentives for monitoring top management and continuing their directorships into a second term, and their weak monitoring incentives, respectively.23We also control forBusy directorandDirector from other province, which measure directors’ busyness and their information disadvantage, respectively. The results do not change.

In columns 7–12, we reestimate the regressions in columns 1–6 using the full sample of proposals and find that our inferences do not change.

As a further test, to mitigate the selection bias pertaining to board formation and proposal selection, we control for proposal fixed effects in the regressions. This approach allows us to examine how the board meeting form affects the dissension incentives of directors with different career concerns. Prior studies show that directors with stronger career concerns are more likely to stand up to management (Masulis and Mobbs 2014;Jiang, Wan, and Zhao 2016). To the extent that the form of board meetings influences director behavior by creating an environment that is more conducive to status equalization and active participation, we expect these results to be more pronounced in cases of remote proposals.

The results using the full sample of proposals are reported in panel B ofTable 5.24Since the regressions control for proposal fixed effects, they include only director characteristics used in panel A as independent variables. We find several important results. First, the coefficient forDirector ageis negative and significant only in the regression using a subsample of remote proposals. This result suggests that remote meetings help young directors, who tend to be less powerful but have stronger career concerns to establish reputations as effective directors, dissent more actively. Second, the coefficient forFirst-term directoris negative and significant only in the subsample of face-to-face meetings. Thus, face-to-face meetings discourage newly hired directors, who have strong career concerns but want to be reappointed for a second term, from expressing their disagreements and concerns freely. Such discouragement is absent in remote meetings. Third, we find that young first-term directors, who have longer career horizons but unsecured future directorships, are more likely to dissent than other directors when they attend remote meetings, suggesting that remote meetings create an environment that encourages young directors to care more about their career concerns than their job continuity. Fourth, consistent with the findings that social connections generate leniency (Jiang, Wan, and Zhao 2016), we find that socially connected directors are less likely to dissent. However, this result is evident only in the subsample of face-to-face meetings, suggesting that socially connected directors face high pressure to conform, particularly when they attend face-to-face meetings. Finally, nonlocal independent directors are more likely to dissent, particularly when they attend face-to-face meetings. Thus, face-to-face meetings, which facilitate discussions among participants, help directors with information disadvantages make more objective judgments.

Overall, these results suggest that independent directors attending (synchronous) remote meetings are more likely to disagree on proposals that require board monitoring than those attending face-to-face meetings, supporting our hypothesis that (synchronous) remote meetings help enhance board monitoring effectiveness. Moreover, remote meetings allow directors with strong career concerns to perform their monitoring function more actively and help overcome the leniency problem of socially connected directors.

4.3 Forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity

As a further test for the effect of remote meetings on directors’ monitoring effectiveness, we focus on the sensitivity of forced CEO turnover to performance. We focus on forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity because firing top executives is considered to be one of the most aggressive actions taken by boards, and turnover-performance sensitivity is one of the most widely used measures of board monitoring effectiveness (e.g.,Weisbach 1988). FollowingChang and Wong (2009), we classify a turnover as routine if the stated reasons in the CSMAR database are retirement, change in controlling shareholders, health, corporate governance reform, or legal disputes. For the remaining turnovers, we trace the destination of the departing CEO and classify a turnover as forced if the CEO takes a new position in the firm that is ranked lower than her former position, leaves the firm to take a new position that is less prestigious than her former position, or leaves the firm to take a managerial position at an unlisted or smaller firm or if we are unable to trace the destination of the departing CEO.25Our final sample consists of 1,270 CEO turnover events, of which 526 are classified as forced. This forced CEO turnover rate (41%⁠) is similar to that inChang and Wong (2009)but higher than the rates in other studies using U.S. firms and firms in other countries. Because the largest shareholders in China hold substantial equity ownership in their firms (mean ownership of 39%in 2019), they have strong incentives to influence managerial decisions by delegating their control and power to the board and to replace CEOs who perform poorly (Jiang and Kim 2015).

Panel A ofTable 6presents the results of logit and conditional logit regressions estimated using board meeting-level data in which the dependent variable is an indicator that equals one if a forced CEO turnover proposal is passed at the board meeting, and zero otherwise. The key independent variable of interest is the interaction term betweenSynchronous remote meeting/Remote meetingandPastindustry-adjusted ROA(i.e., the average quarterly industry-adjusted return on assets (ROA) during the past year immediately prior to the quarter before the meeting date). We control for the same set of meeting-, board-, and firm-specific characteristics as used in the regressions ofTables 4and5. FollowingWeisbach (1988)andFich and Shivdasani (2006), we also control for CEO characteristics (Family CEO,CEO ownership,CEO age, andCEO Tenure). Consistent with the extant literature,Pastindustry-adjusted ROAis significantly negatively related to the likelihood of forced CEO turnover. More importantly, we find that the coefficients for the interaction terms betweenSynchronous remote meetingandPastindustry-adjusted ROAand those on the interaction terms betweenRemote meetingandPastindustry-adjusted ROAare negative and significant in all columns. These results suggest that (synchronous) remote meetings are associated with a higher sensitivity of forced CEO turnover to performance than face-to-face meetings, supporting our hypothesis that (synchronous) remote meetings enhance board monitoring effectiveness.26

To gauge the economic significance of our turnover results, we report the implied probabilities of forced CEO turnover for firms in the top and bottom quartiles (terciles) of firm performance in panel B ofTable 6. The implied probabilities are calculated using the coefficients of the regression in columns 2 and 5 of panel A and averaged across all observations. The first two columns show the results using the subsample of synchronous meetings. The implied probabilities at the 10th, 25th, 75th, and 90th percentiles of firm performance are then calculated by varying only the performance variable (i.e.,Past industry-adjusted ROA) and its interaction withSynchronous remote meeting, while holding all other independent variables constant at their actual values. We find that the effect of firm performance on forced CEO turnover likelihood is larger for firms that hold synchronous remote meetings than for those that hold face-to-face meetings. For example, for firms that hold face-to-face meetings, varyingpast industry-adjusted ROA from the 90th to 10th percentile (i.e., from 1.67%to−1.6%⁠) increases the likelihood of forced CEO turnover by only 0.28%(1.45%–1.17%⁠). In comparison, for firms that hold synchronous remote meetings, varying past industry-adjusted ROA from the 90th to 10th percentile increases the likelihood of CEO turnover by nearly 1.8%(2.46%– 0.66%⁠). This increase of 1.8%in turnover probability is large compared to the unconditional probability of 1%for the synchronous meeting sample. The results are similar when we use the full sample of board meetings.

4.4 Endogeneity tests

Although controlling for various fixed effects in the regressions helps alleviate endogeneity bias resulting from time-invariant omitted variables, it does not address other types of endogeneity problems. For example, time-varying unobservable firm and board characteristics may simultaneously affect both the choice of remote board meetings and board monitoring effectiveness. A firm’s decision to hold a remote meeting could also have its own determinants, thus raising the possibility that firms with certain characteristics choose to hold meetings remotely. Alternatively, as shown inTable 3, director power/convenience could be the primary motive for holding remote meetings, so holding remote meetings may simply reflect an active board’s superior monitoring quality. In this section, we perform several tests, including theHeckman (1979)treatment effect model tests, propensity score matching analyses, firm-by-board fixed effects regressions, subsample analyses, and tests of dissent by powerful directors, to address these concerns.

4.4.1 Heckman treatment effect model

If self-selecting firms are not random subsets of the population, the usual estimators in the regressions will not be consistent (Heckman 1979). To address these self-selection and omitted variable bias problems, we use aHeckman (1979)treatment effect model to estimate the previous regressions inTables 4–6.

In the first-stage regression, the likelihood of a firm’s choice of remote meeting is estimated using a probit regression in which the independent variables are those used in the regressions in column 1 (column 2) ofTables 4and5(Table 6). To meet the inclusion and exclusion requirements in the first stage, we include the board meeting-level variableSevere weatheras the identifying restriction, which is an indicator that equals one if at least one day during the one week (days -3 to 3) surrounding the board meeting date (day 0) has daily precipitation, snowfall, or maximum wind velocity within the top fifth percentile of daily precipitation, snowfall, or maximum wind velocity over the past 30 years, and zero otherwise.27For the dates surrounding the board meeting to be classified as having severe weather, we also require that no date in the previous year during the period from one week before to one week after this year’s board meeting week had severe weather. Given that some regions in China seldom experience any severe rain, snowfall, or wind that could affect public transportation, we follow the criteria developed by the China Meteorological Administration to identify severe rain, snow, and wind and require daily precipitation, snowfall, and maximum wind velocity on board meeting dates to be greater than 25 mm, 5 mm, and 17.2 m/s, respectively, for those dates to be considered as having severe weather. Because these extreme weather conditions are likely to adversely affect the operation of transportation systems and thus to make it difficult for people to travel, we expect such weather conditions to increase the likelihood that firms will hold remote board meetings, satisfying the inclusion requirement. However, because we use weather conditions that are largely unpredictable, our identifying restriction is unlikely to directly influence board monitoring outcomes except via its effect on firms’ choice of remote meetings, thus satisfying the exclusion requirement. To ensure thatSevere weatherdoes not simply capture local economic conditions, predictable weather conditions, or other regional effects, we control for local economic conditions (i.e., local gross domestic product [GDP] and unemployment rate), severe weather patterns (i.e., the natural logarithm of the number of days with severe weather during the previous year), and meteorological station fixed effects, and we replace year and month fixed effects with year-by-month fixed effects in the regressions.

In the second stage, we estimate the regressions ofProportion of independent directors attendedand an indicator for forced CEO turnover (director dissent) onSynchronous remote meeting(Synchronous remote proposal), the inverse Mills ratio (IMR), and other variables used in the first-stage regression. To allow the coefficients for theIMRto vary between synchronous remote meetings (synchronous remote proposals) and face-to-face meetings (face-to-face proposals) and to more precisely interpret its coefficients, we include bothIMR×Synchronous remote meeting(Synchronous remote proposal) andIMR×(1-Synchronous remote meeting(Synchronous remote proposal)) (Tucker 2007,2010;Givoly, Hayn, and Katz 2010). As these interaction terms involving theIMRcorrect for self-selection bias, the coefficient forSynchronous remote meeting(Synchronous remote proposal) measures the pure effect of synchronous remote meetings.

Panel A ofTable 7reports the results using the subsample of synchronous meetings. Columns 1, 3, and 6 report the results of the first-stage regression. As expected, the coefficients forSevere weatherare positive and significant in all three columns. Columns 2, 4, 5, and 7 report the results of the second-stage regression.28Consistent with the results inTables 4–6, we find positive and significant coefficients forSynchronous remote meetingand the interaction term betweenSynchronous remote proposalandMonitoring-related RPT proposal(Monitoring-related all proposal) as well as a negative and significant coefficient for the interaction term betweenSynchronous remote meetingandPast industry-adjusted ROA. The significant coefficient for the interaction term betweenIMRand (1-Synchronous remote proposal) in columns 4 and 5 and that for the interaction term betweenIMRand (1-Synchronous remote meeting) in column 7 suggest the presence of self-selection bias in the tests of the effects of synchronous remote meetings on the likelihoods of dissent and forced CEO turnover. The negative coefficients for these interaction terms further indicate a downward self-selection bias due to an inclination to dissent on the proposal and to replace CEOs in face-to-face meetings. Thus, if the face-to-face meeting were instead held remotely via telephone or video, directors would be more likely to dissent and firms would be more inclined to replace their CEOs.29We reestimate the regressions in panel A ofTable 7using the full sample of board meetings and report the results in panel B ofTable 7. The results are similar.

4.4.2 Do director power/convenience and corporate governance drive the results?

The analysis inTable 3indicates that director power/convenience is an important determinant of the likelihood of remote meetings. Strong directors are not only more willing to resist management but also more likely to suggest the use of remote meetings if they wish to avoid the inconvenience of attending face-to-face meetings. Similarly, well-governed firms are more likely to use remote meetings to perform effective monitoring. Therefore, it is unclear whether remote meetings improve director monitoring or whether stronger directors and firms with better governance prefer remote meetings. In this section, we conduct several analyses to address this issue.

First, we estimate the regressions after controlling for firm-by-board fixed effects, which allows us to measure the within-board variation between board meetings that are held remotely and board meetings that are held face-to-face by the same firm board and to alleviate the concern that time-invariant firm-by-board characteristics (e.g., director power, board governance) drive the results. However, the inclusion of firm-by-board fixed effects does not allow us to control for omitted variable biases resulting from time-variant firm-by-board characteristics. The results using the subsample of synchronous meetings are reported inInternet Appendix Table F. The board is considered the same board if no new director is appointed to the board and no directors depart the board during a certain period of time, and it is considered a different board if there is a change in board composition due to the appointment or departure of at least one independent director. We find that our inferences do not change, suggesting that time-invariant omitted board characteristics are unlikely to drive the observed relation between the selection of synchronous remote meetings and board monitoring effectiveness.

Second, we divide our sample into firms with higher and lower levels of director power/convenience according to the sample median of the first principal component obtained from applying principal component analysis on the director power/convenience variables that are significant in at least two regressions ofTable 3. The results using the subsample of synchronous meetings are reported inInternet Appendix Table G. Our main findings hold for both subsamples, suggesting that director power/convenience is not the driving force for the observed relation between synchronous remote meetings and board monitoring effectiveness.

Third, we divide our sample into firms with higher and lower quality of corporate governance according to the sample median quality of corporate governance. The results using the subsample of synchronous meetings are reported inInternet Appendix Table H. Specifically, we measure the quality of a firm’s governance by the first factor obtained from applying principal component analysis on the key corporate governance variables identified in prior literature, including ownership structure (i.e., ownership by the largest shareholders, top-10 ownership excluding ownership by the largest shareholders, institutional ownership), managerial incentive (i.e., CEO ownership, CEO compensation), managerial power (i.e., CEO-chair duality), and board monitoring (i.e., proportion of independent directors on the board, board size). Our results hold for both subsamples, except in column 5.31
article.5. Additional Tests:
In this section, we conduct several additional tests to provide further evidence regarding the effects of remote meetings on board monitoring effectiveness. We briefly summarize the results of these tests below.

5.1 Market reactions to M&A announcements

If remote meetings indeed allow directors to perform more effective monitoring, to the extent that this improved monitoring translates into better investment decisions and higher firm value, the investment decisions made at remote meetings should be more value-enhancing than those made at face-to-face meetings. To test this prediction, we use proposal-level data to investigate whether M&As approved at remote meetings result in higher announcement returns than those approved at face-to-face meetings. Because both the monitoring and advisory functions of directors are important for value-increasing M&A decisions (Schmidt 2015), to examine the effect of remote meetings on director monitoring, we focus mainly onRPT M&As in which agency conflicts are particularly severe; thus, independent directors’ monitoring is important.

For each M&A event, we use the date when the M&A proposal is announced in the public notice on the resolutions of the board meeting as the announcement date. We exclude M&A events if other major confounding corporate events (e.g., appointment/dismissal of top executives and chairpersons, dividend payments, and earnings disclosures) are discussed during the event window from one day before to one day after the M&A announcement date. We restrict the sample to M&As in which the acquiring firm obtains more than 50%of the target equity. These sample selection criteria yield a final sample of 471 (922) completed M&A events discussed at synchronous board meetings (board meetings) from 2006 to 2019. To examine market reactions to M&A announcements, we employ a standard event study methodology and compute the cumulative abnormal return from one day before the announcement date to one day after the announcement date (CAR (⁠−1, 1)). We use the CSMAR value-weighted stock return as the market portfolio return. Our results do not change when we use CAR (⁠−1, 0) or CAR (0) as the dependent variable. In addition to controlling for the board meeting and board characteristics used in previous tables, we include several deal and firm characteristics in the regressions following prior literature (e.g., Masulis, Wang and Xie 2007;Moeller, Schlingemann, and Stulz 2005;Cai and Sevilir 2012). The deal characteristics included are the relative size of the deal (Relative deal size), the method of payment (Cash deal,Stock deal), an indicator for the acquirer and the target operating in different industries (Diversifying acquisitions), an indicator for both the acquirer and the target operating in a high-tech industry (High-tech deal), and an indicator for the public target (Public target). We control for several firm characteristics, including acquiring firms’Leverage,Size,Tobin’s q,SOE,Top-1 ownership,Cash flow, and past stock return (Stock price run-up).

Table 8reports the results from ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions in which the dependent variable is the CAR (-1, 1). In columns 1 and 2, we use the subsample of synchronous meetings when estimating the regressions. We find that the coefficients for the interaction term betweenSynchronousremote meetingandRPT M&Aare positive and significant in both regressions, suggesting that the valuation effect of related-party M&A deals is more positive when they are approved at synchronous remote meetings. Using the full sample of meetings in columns 3 and 4 does not change the results. These results further support our hypothesis that directors at board meetings held remotely play a more effective monitoring role than directors at face-to-face meetings.32In untabulated tests, to alleviate the concern that M&A deals voted in these remote meetings are systematically different from those that are voted in face-to-face meetings, we estimate the regressions inTable 8using aHeckman (1979)treatment effect model and find consistent results.

As a further test of remote meetings’ monitoring efficiency, using firm-year data we examine whether boards make better investment decisions when investment-related proposals are discussed at (synchronous) remote meetings. To the extent that (synchronous) remote meetings help improve board monitoring effectiveness, we expect that an overinvestment proposal is less likely to pass at a (synchronous) remote meeting, and thus the proportion of investment-related proposals discussed in (synchronous) remote meetings is negatively related to a firm’s investment inefficiency. As discussed and shown inInternet Appendix K, we find results that are consistent with this prediction.

5.2 Remote meeting type

Our analyses thus far show that all remote meetings have the same effects on board monitoring effectiveness regardless of their communication type. However, such effects are likely to differ depending on communication type. For example, attendants at synchronous remote meetings conducted via video and telephone are required to attend the meeting at a specific time, and they can communicate with each other synchronously. In contrast, attendants at asynchronous remote meetings conducted via email or fax cannot see or hear each other. Therefore, it is likely that asynchronous remote meetings are least affected by intimidation, social hierarchy, and the pressure to conform, followed by synchronous remote meetings and face-to-face meetings.

To address this issue, we examine whether directors’ meeting attendance behavior, the likelihood of director dissent, and the likelihood of forced CEO turnover differ across asynchronous remote meetings, synchronous remote meetings, and face-to-face meetings. We do not include an indicator for face-to-face meetings in the regressions and use them as the reference group for comparison. The results are reported inTable 9. We find that compared to face-to-face meetings, both synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings are associated with higher meeting attendance records of independent directors, higher likelihood of director dissent on monitoring-related proposals, and higher forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity. However, there are no significant differences in these outcome variables between synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings except that director attendance records are higher in asynchronous remote meetings than in synchronous remote meetings. Given that both synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings significantly reduce the observability of social context cues and increase the confidence of outside directors by alleviating their “away” field psychology (Pierce, Kostova, and Dirks 2003;Porteous 1976), these results indicate that remote meetings, regardless of whether they are held synchronously or asynchronously, create environments for objective judgment and help improve board monitoring effectiveness. More detailed discussions of these results are presented inInternet Appendix L.
