5.3 Synchronous remote meetings identified by the machine learning approach

Our previous analyses based on the subsample of synchronous meetings focus only on remote meetings whose commination type can be identified precisely and exclude meetings whose commination type is not known. To determine whether our results are robust to the inclusion of unknown types of remote meetings that we can identify as synchronous remote meetings using the machine learning approach, we include these synchronous remote meetings in the subsample of synchronous meetings and reestimate the regressions inTables 4–6.Table 10presents the results. To the extent that firms exhibit certain patterns of reporting when describing synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings in their public notices, we expect that the machine learning approach enables us to identify such patterns reliably. Specifically, we retrieve the textual paragraph describing the board meeting form from the firm’s public notice and vectorize this textual information with a term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) term-weighting technique.33We then use the sample of 6,336 remote meetings whose communication types are known to train a classification model to categorize meetings as either synchronous or asynchronous remote meetings by applying the decision-tree machine learning approach.34Finally, we use the trained decision-tree classification model to predict and categorize remote meetings whose precise communication types are unknown into synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings. Adding these remote meetings that are predicted to be synchronous to the synchronous remote meetings that we know exact communication type, our new sample of synchronous meetings used inTable 10consists of 41,977 (236,952) board meeting (proposal) observations. We find that our results remain the same, except in column 5.

article.6. Summary and Conclusion:
In this paper, we investigate which firms are more likely to hold synchronous remote meetings and how these meetings affect board monitoring effectiveness. Using manually collected unique data on the board meeting type of Chinese firms, we find that urgency and director power/convenience are the primary motivations for synchronous remote meetings held via telephone or video. Specifically, synchronous remote board meetings are more likely to be held to discuss proposals on short notice. They are also more likely to be held by firms experiencing large decline in performance and boards whose independent directors are more powerful or require more convenience. On the other hand, face-to-face meetings are more likely to be held for meetings with a large number of proposals and for annual board meetings, suggesting that these meetings are used mainly to discuss scheduled issues.

Consistent with our argument that synchronous remote meetings help alleviate status inequality among directors and their pressure for conformity, thus increasing their monitoring incentives and effectiveness, we find that compared to face-to-face meetings, synchronous remote meetings are associated with independent directors’ improved meeting attendance behavior, higher likelihood of director dissent on monitoring-related proposals, and higher forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity. These results are robust to the use of aHeckman (1979)treatment effect model in which severe weather is used as the identifying restriction. We also find that related-party M&A deals are greeted more positively when they are approved at synchronous remote meetings and that firms’ investment inefficiency decreases as more investment-related proposals are approved at synchronous remote meetings during a fiscal year. All these results hold when we replace synchronous remote meetings with remote meetings that include both synchronous and asynchronous remote meetings.

Overall, our study provides novel evidence regarding board monitoring by utilizing previously unexplored data on board meeting type and shows that (synchronous) remote meetings increase board monitoring effectiveness.

Footnotes

1 See, for example, Weisbach (1988) , Byrd and Hickman (1992) , and Klein (2002) , all of whom examine the effects of board independence on firm performance and policies, as well as Knyazeva, Knyazeva, and Raheja (2013) , Coles, Daniel, and Naveen (2015) , Bernile, Bhagwat, and Yonker (2018) , and Kang, Kim, and Oh (2022) , all of whom examine the effect of board diversity on firm performance.

2 Remote board meetings held via video, telephone, email, or fax and that thus allow for the physical separation of participants during the session have become increasingly popular in recent years. For example, only 32 % of the Chinese firms included in our sample held their board meetings remotely in 2006, but this number increased to 70 % in 2019, the last year included in our sample period.

3 Attendants of remote meetings held via email or fax cannot see or hear each other and those at remote meetings held by telephone or video can observe each other’s social context cues only partially. For example, in remote meetings that are held via telephone, visual images are absent and attendants may not hear each other’s voices clearly or be able to clarify the exact identity of the speakers when more than one attendant speaks simultaneously. In remote meetings that are held via video, although attendants can partially observe each other’s visual signals, they may not be able to observe each other’s entire bodily gestures since the video screen usually captures only certain part of attendants’ bodies. Attendants may also experience difficulties in making direct eye contact with each other depending on camera placement ( Fullwood 2007 ; Basch et al. 2021 ).

4 For example, directors attending face-to-face meetings can exchange their information in a richer way and be more engaged than those attending remote meetings, which is likely to result in more information flow to directors and more scrutiny of managers.

5 Directors attending remote meetings have the same legal responsibilities as those attending face-to-face meetings with respect to the proposals on which they vote, and directors in general work diligently to develop their reputations as effective directors ( Fama 1980 ; Fama and Jensen 1983 ). Thus, directors attending remote meetings have strong incentives to overcome the potential information disadvantages they face by reading and assessing the provided materials thoroughly prior to voting and by obtaining all relevant information that can help them make effective decisions.

6 See Hadid (2014) .

7 In the United States, detailed information about board meeting activities, including the form of the meeting, is unavailable, except for information on the number of meetings held per year and the names of directors who attended fewer than 75 % of these meetings, which is required by the Securities and Exchange Commission’s Regulation S-K (Item 407(i)).

8 The Shanghai and Shenzhen Stock Exchanges jointly launched the CSI300 index on April 8, 2005, to track the overall performance of the Chinese A-share market. This capitalization-weighted stock index consists of 300 firms listed on these two exchanges and is adjusted twice a year. Firms are selected based on their market capitalization and liquidity. The total market capitalization of the firms included in the CSI300 index accounted for 59 % of the market capitalization of all firms listed on the Shanghai and Shenzhen Stock Exchanges in 2019.

9 We exclude firms in financial service industries from the analysis because the financing decisions and accounting treatments of these firms significantly differ from those in other industries. As shown in Internet Appendix Table A , the inclusion of such firms in the analysis does not change our results.

10 We require the public notice to mention both the meeting time and the meeting location for a remote meeting to be classified as a synchronous remote meeting because video/telephone meetings usually require a specific meeting time and an email or a fax meeting does not need a specific location. We consider a remote meeting as a synchronous remote meeting if a firm’s public notice mentions the terms “host” and “coattending” because an email or a fax meeting does not need a host or coattender.

11 In Section 5.3 , we further classify remote meetings whose communication type is unknown as either synchronous or asynchronous remote meetings using the machine learning approach. Adding the synchronous remote meetings identified by the machine learning approach to the synchronous meeting sample does not change our results.

12 Our private interviews with several directors of Chinese firms indicate that face-to-face meetings usually last longer than remote meetings. The longer time spent in face-to-face meetings is unsurprising because a larger number of proposals are discussed at these meetings, and more time is spent on formalities, such as a welcome address from the chairperson, the reading of the proposals by the board secretary, and polite remarks from the directors. In contrast, less time is spent on such formalities in remote meetings because such meetings do not feature a host or proposal readings. At remote meetings, directors usually speak if they have questions or suggestions. Although remote meetings tend to be shorter than face-to-face meetings, time spent on the discussion of each proposal is not necessarily shorter in remote meetings. We thank the directors of Chinese firms, including Ping Jiang, Zhongwei Luo, Yun Sun, Huacheng Wang, Yutao Wang, and Min Zhang, for sharing their experiences with us.

13 To determine whether the significance of the proposals under discussion differs between remote and face-to-face meetings, we also examine the distribution of the types of proposals discussed at these meetings in Internet Appendix Table C . We find that three types of proposals (i.e., investment, M&A, restructuring, and asset sales; director and officer selection, appointment, turnover, and compensation; and internal corporate bylaws and internal meeting agendas), which are likely to be strongly associated with firms’ important strategic decisions, account for 56 % ⁠ , 55 % ⁠ , and 43 % of the proposals discussed at remote meetings, synchronous remote meetings, and face-to-face meetings, respectively. These results suggest that a large proportion of the proposals discussed at remote meetings are not mundane but are rather quite important.

14 The variable Drop in performance equals one if the firm’s cumulative market-adjusted stock return during the one month before the meeting notice date is lower than − 10 % ⁠ , and zero otherwise.

15 Duchin and Sosyura (2021) focus on the efficacy of remote working arrangements and show that long-distance CEOs experience a loss of information because of their disconnection from the firm’s daily operations.

16 To more precisely capture the manager power/convenience measured by CEO-chair duality , we replace CEO-Chair duality with Professional CEO-chair duality , which equals one if the CEO who is not a member of the controlling family serves as the chair of the board, and zero otherwise. We also replace CEO-Chair duality with Professional CEO-chair duality and Family CEO-chair duality , which equals one if the CEO who is a member of the controlling family serves as the chair of the board, and zero otherwise. In untabulated tests, we find that the coefficients for these variables are insignificant.

17 The firm-by-director fixed effects absorb the indicator for male directors.

18 Of 341 proposals, 10 receive both “abstain” and “against” votes. To avoid double counting, we count each of these 10 proposals as receiving only one dissent vote, resulting in a final sample of 331 proposals that receive dissent votes from independent directors. Jiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016) find that 92 % of proposals in their sample eventually pass despite dissent, and they argue that director dissent in China is mainly for evaluating management and disseminating value-relevant information to the public, not for directly altering the firm’s course of action. Similar to their findings, we find that 96 % of the proposals in our sample that receive a dissenting vote eventually pass.

19 Previous studies show that related party transactions are one of the most widely used forms of insider self-dealing by controlling shareholders in Asian firms; these transactions allow related parties to divert corporate resources to themselves via unfair transfer prices, loan guarantees, or other self-serving financial transactions, and they adversely affect firm value and operating performance ( Bae, Kang, and Kim 2002 ; Cheung, Rau, and Stouraitis 2006 ; Jiang, Lee, and Yue 2010 ).

20 The Code of Corporate Governance for Listed Companies in China in 2002 stipulates that the audit committee, the nomination committee, and the remuneration and appraisal committee should be chaired by independent directors and that independent directors should constitute the majority of these committees. Similarly, Faleye, Hoitash, and Hoitash (2011) classify board committees in U.S. firms into two types—committees that play a monitoring role (audit, compensation, and nominating/corporate governance committees) and committees that play an advisory role (strategy, investment, finance, and executive committees)—and show that directors on monitoring committees enhance the effectiveness of board monitoring. For investment-related proposals, we categorize only those regarding transactions with related parties as Monitoring-related RPT proposal . We do not categorize other investment-related proposals as monitoring-related proposals because certain investment-related proposals (e.g., R&D investments) are more likely to require advice from directors and should thus be categorized as advisory proposals.

21 We expect remote meetings to influence director voting mainly in cases of monitoring-related proposals because board decisions on these proposals are likely to have a significant effect on corporate insiders who also serve as directors on boards. In contrast, other proposals that do not involve related parties are unlikely to have a significant direct effect on corporate insiders because the private benefits that these insiders can obtain from the passage of such proposals are expected to be relatively small.

22 Following Jiang, Wan, and Zhao (2016) , we measure directors’ professional backgrounds using indicators for whether the director has worked for an academic institution ( Director academic ), whether the director is a former government official ( Director bureaucrat ), whether the director is an accounting professional ( Director accountant ), whether the director is a legal professional ( Director lawyer ), whether the director has worked for a financial institution ( Director banking ), and whether the director has worked for a nonfinancial institution as a non-CEO and nonchair executive ( Director executive ). The indicator for male directors and Director education are absorbed by firm-by-director fixed effects.

23 Independent directors of listed firms in China are limited to two terms in the same firm, where one term typically lasts for 3 years. Directors in their first term thus have strong incentives to please insiders to ensure their reappointment for a second term ( Jiang, Wan, and Zhao 2016 ).

24 We do not conduct the analyses using a subsample of proposals discussed at synchronous meetings since the sample size for synchronous remote proposals that receive dissent votes from at least one, but not all, independent directors is too small to conduct meaningful analyses.

25 The CSMAR database classifies CEO turnovers into twelve categories: (1) job change, (2) retirement, (3) contract expiration, (4) change in controlling shareholder, (5) resignation, (6) dismissal, (7) health, (8) personal reasons, (9) corporate governance reform, (10) legal disputes, (11) completion of acting duties, and (12) no reason given. We treat CEO turnover whose stated reason is corporate governance reform as routine because we find that in these cases, the departing CEO either retains her position as board chairperson or is appointed to a key managerial position at the parent firm. We treat CEO turnover whose stated reason is a legal dispute as routine because it is typically the result of court enforcement, not of a firm’s monitoring activity. We trace the destination of departing CEOs using various data sources, including firms’ annual reports, company websites, and other internet information ( http://www.baidu.com , http://www.sina.com.cn ). We define CEO turnover in which the information on the destination of the departing CEO is unavailable as forced because it is unlikely that there would be no information regarding the departing CEO if she had accepted a better position than her previous CEO position at a listed firm ( Chang and Wong 2009 ).

26 If the use of (synchronous) remote meetings increases monitoring effectiveness mainly through boards’ quick response to urgent issues, our results for higher forced CEO turnover-performance sensitivity in (synchronous) remote meetings than in face-to-face meetings could be because of this quick response, not necessarily because of improved board monitoring effectiveness. To address this concern, we conduct two untabulated tests. First, we reestimate the regressions in Tables 4 – 6 after adding Interval to the regressions to control for urgency. Second, we reestimate the regressions in Tables 4 – 6 after excluding firms whose Drop in performance (indicator) takes the value of one from the sample. We find that our results do not change.

27 Prior meteorological studies have usually used the top fifth percentile of daily precipitation and/or temperature when defining extreme weather (e.g., Zhai et al. 2005 ; Alexander et al. 2006 ).

28 Since applying Heckman (1979) model requires the assumption of normal errors in the second stage, we replace the logit model with a probit model in the second-stage regression when estimating the likelihoods of dissent and forced CEO turnover.

29 Alternatively, the negative coefficients for these interaction terms suggest an upward self-selection bias for face-to-face meetings, indicating that certain firm or board characteristics that increase the likelihood of holding face-to-face meetings (e.g., good firm performance) increase the likelihoods of dissent and forced CEO turnover. This interpretation is consistent with the downward self-selection bias for synchronous remote meetings that certain firm/board characteristics that increase the likelihood of holding synchronous remote meetings (e.g., poor firm performance) decrease the likelihoods of dissent and forced CEO turnover.

30 To further address the selection bias resulting from observable omitted variables, we employ a propensity score matching approach in which we use board characteristics that are significant in at least two regressions in Table 3 and other board meeting and firm characteristics as the matching variables. Internet Appendix Table E reports the results. Panel A shows that none of the characteristics used in the matching differs significantly between the treatment sample (synchronous remote meetings (proposals)) and the control sample (face-to-face meetings (proposals)) except for Average tenure of independent directors and Proposal number in the proposal-level matched sample, indicating that we identify face-to-face meetings (proposals) that are highly similar to synchronous remote meetings (proposals). Panel B presents results of the regressions in Tables 4 – 6 using the propensity-score matched sample. The results are almost identical.

31 Directors’ willingness to dissent on proposals is also likely to differ across firms with different levels of managerial power ( Malenko 2013 ). Moreover, the proportion of dissent votes observed in the subsample of synchronous meetings is fairly low (0.11 % ⁠ ). To alleviate these concerns, in untabulated tests, we use only a subsample of firms whose directors have ever dissented within a certain period during which the identity of CEOs and chairpersons does not change. Focusing on this subsample significantly increases the proportion of dissent votes observed in the subsample of synchronous meetings from 0.11 % to 2.4 % ⁠ . Our results do not change.

32 Some important proposals that require directors’ long-term discussion and time commitment, such as M&A and CEO firing proposals, may be discussed at several meetings prior to voting. To the extent that the director monitoring of these proposals is conducted across several meetings and the decisions made at board meetings represent the cumulative outcomes of the discussions from these meetings, it is possible that when such decisions are reached, the type of meeting is of little consequence, except in the timing. To address this concern, using the subsample of synchronous meetings, we identify proposals that are revised from previous meetings and conduct several tests. First, we examine market reactions to M&A announcements after excluding revised M&A proposals that have been discussed at previous meetings from the sample. As an additional test, we repeat the analysis after excluding M&A proposals if the meetings occurred within the past quarter also discussed any M&A proposal because these proposals are more likely to be the same proposals discussed at different meetings. Second, with regard to CEO turnover analyses, we repeat the analyses in Table 6 after excluding the meeting if it includes revised turnover proposals that have been discussed at previous meetings. We also repeat the analyses after excluding the meeting if it includes turnover proposals and the previous meetings that occurred within the past quarter also discussed any turnover proposal. Third, we repeat the analyses in Table 4 after excluding the meeting if it includes revised proposals that have been discussed at previous meetings and the meeting if it includes proposals on same topics that were discussed at the previous meetings occurred within the past quarter, respectively. Similarly, we repeat the analyses in Table 5 after excluding revised proposals that have been discussed at previous meetings and proposals on same topics that were discussed at the previous meetings occurred within the past quarter, respectively. Internet Appendix Tables I and J show that our results remain unchanged.

33 The TF-IDF term-weighting technique is a popular technique used for information retrieval ( Loughran and McDonald 2011 ). It is based on the idea that words that appear more frequently in one document and less frequently in other documents are more useful and important for textual analysis and should thus be given more weight when constructing the vector. Using the TF-IDF technique, we transform the textual description of each remote meeting observation into a 1,402-dimensional vector, featuring key words as dimensions and the TF-IDF value of each word as the weight.

34 Decision tree is a predictive modeling approach that is widely used in machine learning ( Mullainathan and Spiess 2017 ). Specifically, the decision-tree classification model uses a decision tree as a prediction model to go from observations (e.g., the 1,402-dimensional vector computed using the TF-IDF technique) to conclusions regarding the item’s target value (e.g., the class label for synchronous and asynchronous remote meeting). Using the randomly selected 80 % and the remaining 20 % in-sample observations as the train set and the test set, respectively, the decision-tree classification model offers 99 % precision in predicting an actual synchronous remote meeting correctly as a synchronous remote meeting.
